mnemonics.title: OSI Layer Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away - Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application (bottom to top)

mnemonics.title: TCP/IP Layers
mnemonics.content: TINA - Transport, Internet, Network Access (from top to bottom)

mnemonics.title: Layer Functions Quick Recall
mnemonics.content: P-D-N-T-S-P-A: Please Don't Nod Throw Sausage Pizza Away - Physical(1), Data(2), Network(3), Transport(4), Session(5), Presentation(6), Application(7)

acronyms.usage: Reference model for network communication standardization
Actual protocol suite used in the internet
Data unit at each OSI layer: Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bits
Physical address at Data Link layer, 48-bit hardware address
Network layer protocol for logical addressing and routing
Application layer protocol for web communication
Application layer protocol for file transfers

visualTips.visualization: Draw 7-layer OSI on left, 4-layer TCP/IP on right. Map: OSI Physical+DataLink = TCP/IP Network Access; OSI Network = TCP/IP Internet; OSI Transport = TCP/IP Transport; OSI Session+Presentation+Application = TCP/IP Application. Use different colors for each mapping.

Think of a Russian nesting doll. Largest doll = Application Data. Each smaller doll added = header from each layer (Transport segment, Network packet, Data Link frame). At Physical layer, it's converted to bits (electrical signals). Reverse at receiving end.
Draw horizontal flow: [Data] → [Segment] → [Packet] → [Frame] → [Bits]. Above each, write layer: Application→Transport→Network→DataLink→Physical. Arrow direction shows encapsulation going down.
Layer 1 (Physical): Hub/Repeater; Layer 2 (Data Link): Switch/Bridge; Layer 3 (Network): Router. Draw stacked boxes labeled L1, L2, L3 to visualize which devices process at each layer.

analogies.analogy: Application Layer = Writing the letter; Presentation = Putting in envelope/translation; Session = Tracking the mail; Transport = Postal service guarantee; Network = Determining delivery route; Data Link = Local post office sorting; Physical = The actual truck/car delivering letters.

analogies.concept: TCP vs UDP - Two Delivery Methods
analogies.analogy: TCP is like calling your friend and confirming they received your package before hanging up. UDP is like sending a text message - you send it but don't check if it was received. Both deliver messages but with different reliability levels.

You have a message (gift). You wrap it in a box (Transport segment), put it in a shipping container (Network packet), load on a truck (Data Link frame), and send as physical signals. At each step, new packaging is added (encapsulated). Reverse at destination (decapsulated).

MAC address is like a person's permanent identity (DNA) - never changes. IP address is like their current postal address - can change depending on location. MAC works locally (neighborhood), IP works globally (across cities).
